title: Explain spawn_blocking bridge idiom in client_task.rs
description: client_task.rs lines 54-64 spawn a spawn_blocking task to forward std::sync::mpsc commands into a tokio channel. The comment says 'bridge the blocking std receiver to an async tokio channel' but does not explain *why* this is necessary. Without context: egui's App::update() runs on the GUI thread (synchronous), so the UI sender is std::sync::mpsc; but the client task lives inside a tokio Runtime where calling blocking rx.recv() directly in an async context would stall the executor thread. A reader 12 months from now will not know whether this could be replaced with a tokio::sync::mpsc directly, or why the two-channel approach was chosen. Add a comment explaining: (1) why std::sync::mpsc is used on the UI side (egui is not async), and (2) why a spawn_blocking bridge is needed rather than a shared tokio channel.
